segments.text: Hey everyone, before I jump in, let me
give you a little context on where I'm
coming from. I've been in tech for about
30 years now. Most of that time has been
in software engineering and enterprise
architecture. These days I run my own
consulting agency KOFT where we help
organizations figure out how to actually
get value from AI through strong
engineering and architecture practices.
And I still write code every day, every
week. I'm in the editor right alongside
the teams I work with. So when I talk
about what AI assisted development looks
like daytoday, I'm not speaking from the
sidelines. I'm living it. And what I'm
seeing across teams and organizations
right now is something I think a lot of
you are feeling too. So let me ask you
something. Think about the last time you
built a new service or built out a new
feature from scratch. A couple of years
ago, you'd open your editor, pull up a
template maybe, and start writing. Line
by line, you picked the patterns. You
made the decisions, and you understood
what you were building because you were
the one building it. Now, you type a few
sentences into a chat window, and
something that looks like finished code
shows up. Sometimes it's finished. It
may complete the test and it goes
straight into a poll request. Done.
You're on to your next ticket. That's
just how we work now. AI assisted
development isn't some pilot program
anymore. It's real and the speed is
exciting. Your sprint charts, I bet they
look amazing. I bet your leadership is
happy and everyone feels like it's
working. But then a few weeks go by and
somebody on your team pulls up a file
and goes, "Wait, what is this doing?"
The code works. Sure, it's running in
production, but nobody fully gets why it
works. the patterns don't match the rest
of your code base and there are
assumptions baked in that nobody talked
about because nobody wrote it and AI did
and now that developer is spending their
whole sprint untangling something that
took 30 seconds to create. That's what I
want to talk about today. AI changed how
fast we write code, but it didn't change
who's responsible for what ships. And
that gap between speed and ownership,
that's where things start to break. Some
people call this vibe coding. I'm sure
you've heard of that term. You describe
what you want. The AI gives you
something. You glance at it. It looks
right. You ship it. The vibe is good.
the code feels done and for prototypes
and quick experiments that workflow
typically works great. But when
vibecoded output starts flowing into
production systems into shared code
bases into services that other teams
depend on that's when the cost shows up
because something that feels done and
something that is done are two totally
different things.
Now, I could spend the next 20 minutes
walking through adoption stats and
productivity studies, but you've
probably heard a few of those already
today. So, let's skip ahead to the part
that nobody's measuring yet. What all
this speed is actually costing your
team.
We all know what technical debt is. You
take a shortcut, you know you're taking
it, and you tell yourself, "We'll come
back and clean this up."
That's a trade-off you made on purpose,
and sometimes it's the right call.
Quality debt, though, is a different
thing. It sneaks in. It shows up when
code enters your codebase that works
fine, passes your test, and looks
totally reasonable on the surface, but
it's carrying hidden gaps. The intent
behind the code is fuzzy. The patterns
don't match what your team normally
does. Edge cases are handled in ways
that seem fine until they blow up on a
Wednesday night at 2:00 a.m. And I'm
speaking from experience. When you write
code by hand, you leave your
fingerprints, your naming conventions,
your comments, even the way you
structure your logic tells the next
person something about what you are
trying to solve. AI generated code
doesn't have fingerprints. It gives you
something that looks right. It gives you
working syntax, but it doesn't give you
context. And when you multiply that
across your whole team, hundreds of PRs,
thousands of lines of generated code
every week, you end up with a code base
that people stop trusting. Reviews take
longer because there's more to doublech
checkck. And those hidden assumptions,
they start showing up as production
incidents. That's quality debt. And most
teams don't even have a way to see it
yet. A simple way to think about it is
technical debt is like putting something
on a credit card on purpose. You know
the bill is coming, but quality debt is
like someone else using your credit card
without telling you. The charges keep
adding up and you don't even know until
the statement arrives.
Okay, let me walk you through something
that might sound familiar. Your team
started using AI coding tools about six
months ago. Output went up. More PRs
getting merged every sprint. You shared
the numbers with the leadership.
Everyone high-fived and you moved on.
But then a few things started to shift
quietly.
Code reviews started taking longer. Not
because your reviewers got worse at
their jobs, but because there was way
more to look at and way less context to
go on. Developers started spending more
time reading generated code than writing
their own. And some of your best
engineers started saying things like, "I
spent all day refactoring something we
merged two weeks ago." That's the rework
tax. is the cost your team pays after
the code ships and it doesn't show up on
any dashboard. Let me give you a
specific example. Say your team
generates a service integration with an
AI tool. It works. It passed the test.
It gets merged. Three weeks later
though, someone on another team tries to
build on top of that integration and
discovers the generated code assumed a
synchronous pattern that doesn't fit the
rest of your system.
Now you've got a refactor nobody planned
for because that original task was
already marked completed. Or picture
this, a junior developer uses an AI tool
to build an API endpoint. The code looks
clean. It follows conventions from the
model's training data, but it doesn't
follow your team's conventions, your
naming patterns, your error handling
approach, your logging standards. None
of that is there. So, a senior engineer
spends two hours in review walking
through every single thing that's off.
The PR goes through three rounds before
it finally merges. This is happening
everywhere, right now. And I am speaking
from experience. And do you want to know
the frustrating part? Your regular
metrics won't catch it. If you're
tracking velocity by story points or
PR's merge, you look more productive
than you've ever been. That rework just
gets absorbed into future sprints as
unplanned work. So, let me frame it this
way. If your team is shipping 40% more
code but spending 60% more time on
rework, where did the productivity go?
If reviews are taking twice as long and
you're refactoring code that's less than
a month old, are you actually moving
faster or are you just churning? That's
the questions most teams haven't asked
yet. I've had this conversation with
engineering leaders who tell me, "My
team has never been more productive."
And then I ask about their defect rates
on recently merged code or how many
sprint points went to unplanned work
last quarter and the room gets quiet
because those numbers tell a completely
different story. The rework tax builds
up quietly in review comments that keep
getting longer. In standups where
someone says, "Yeah, I'm still working
through that integration from last
sprint." In the senior engineer who
quietly rewrites a generated module on a
Friday afternoon because it's faster
than explaining what's wrong with it.
That's the tax and your team is already
paying it.
So, what do the teams that are actually
thriving with AI tools have in common?
It's a few things, and they are simpler
than you think. First, they treat every
piece of AI output as a draft every
time. This sounds like a no-brainer, but
look at how most teams actually work.
The AI spits out the code, it compiles,
the developer skims it, maybe changes a
variable name, and opens a PR. There's
this unspoken assumption that generated
code is closer to done than code you
wrote yourself. Strong teams flip that.
They treat generated code the way you
treat a first draft from a brand new
team member. It needs a real look. It
needs context and it needs to match how
this specific team builds software.
Second, they make intent visible. When
someone on the team uses AI to generate
code, they write down what they asked
for, what they changed, and why, not as
busy work, because the next person who
touches that code needs to understand
the thinking behind it. And if that
thinking only lives in the prompt that
generated the code, it's gone the second
the chat window closes. Third, they push
quality checks way earlier in the
process. This is the big one. If your
quality gates are at the PR stage or
later, you've already stacked up debt.
The code is written. The developer has
mentally moved on. The reviewer is now
doing cleanup work that should have been
caught before a commit ever happened.
Strong teams use tools that give
feedback right in the editor in real
time. Not just linting, pattern checks,
naming conventions, consistency with the
existing code base. Developers see the
issue in seconds, not days later in a
review comment. I worked with one team
that did exactly this. They added
automated quality checks that run on
every file save. Their PR rejection rate
dropped by half. And the part that
surprised them, the developers actually
lagged it. They stopped getting
blindsided by review feedback three days
after they moved on to something else.
The loop got shorter. The cost of fixing
things went down and reviewers could
actually focus on design and logic
instead of chasing style and convention
issues. That's what shifting quality
left looks like. You catch problems when
they're cheap to fix.
Okay, so this is where I want to
challenge how most of us are thinking
about this because the metrics we've
been using for years, they're starting
to lie to us. Velocity has always been a
go-to signal. How much work is the team
getting done per sprint? How fast are
features moving through the pipeline?
Now, those numbers still matter, but
they're not enough anymore. When AI
tools can generate code at machine
speed, raw output stops being the
bottleneck. The real question is whether
that output holds up over time. So, what
should you actually be tracking? Start
with the rework rate. How often is code
getting changed within 30 days of being
merged? If a file gets modified,
patched, or refactored shortly after it
ships, that's a signal. It means the
first version didn't meet the bar, even
though it passed review. Track this for
AI assisted code versus everything else,
and patterns will jump out at you. Some
teams find that generated code gets
reworked two to three times more often
than code written by hand, which tells
you something about where the guard
rails need to be. Next, look at how your
team's time breaks down between building
new things and fixing recent things.
Most teams don't track this on purpose,
but it tells you everything. If 40% of
your sprint is going to unplanned
rework, bug fixes on new code, and
refactors that weren't on anyone's road
map, your team is not building. They're
cleaning up after themselves. Then look
at your code reviews. If review cycles
are getting longer and PRs are bouncing
back and forth more, that's a quality
signal. It means the code showing up in
review isn't meeting the team standards
and your reviewers are picking up the
slack. That burns people out and it hits
your most experienced engineers first
because they're the ones doing the heavy
lifting and review.
Last one. When a bug makes it to
production, trace it back. Was it
generated code? Did the AI introduce a
pattern that doesn't fit your
architecture?
Was there an edge case the tool just
didn't think about? That kind of
traceability tells you exactly where
your process is leaking.
Now, let's get to the good part. You
don't need some big new platform to
start doing this. Most teams already
have the data sitting right there. Your
version control system knows when the
files change right after a merge. Your
project tracker knows how much unplanned
work showed up in each sprint. Your
review tools know how long PRs take and
how many rounds they go through. The
data exists. Someone just needs to
connect the dots. So my challenge to
you, pick one metric and I'd start with
rework rate. Measure it for 60 days.
Compare AI assisted code to everything
else. That single number will tell you
more about your team's real engineering
health than any productivity report
you're looking at today. And I want to
be real about something. Quality debt is
a leadership problem, a systems problem.
If your organization is rewarding speed,
tracking output volume, and celebrating
how fast teams adopted AI tools, you are
building the conditions for quality debt
to pile up. Your developers will do what
the system rewards. And right now, most
systems reward shipping fast. So if
you're an engineering leader or an
architect, your job is to set up
workflows where quality is the easy
path, the default path. That means
putting tools in place that gives
developers feedback before code review,
not after. It means writing down your
quality standards and automating them,
so they don't just live in a senior
engineer's head. It means looking at how
you evaluate team performance and making
sure things like rework rate, code
health, and maintainability actually
carry weight alongside velocity. And it
means looking at what your best
engineers are actually doing all day. If
your strongest people are spending their
time reviewing AI generated code instead
of designing systems and mentoring the
next generation of engineers, you've got
a misalignment. You're paying senior
level salaries for review work that
tooling and process should be handling.
Think about it this way. When was the
last time a promotion conversation
included how well someone kept quality
high across their team's AI assisted
output? When did you last recognize
someone for catching a pattern problem
before it spread into the codebase? If
the answer is never, your incentives are
telling your team exactly what matters
and they're listening. The team that
gets this right builds environments
where AI makes their best people even
better instead of burying them in
cleanup work. That's a design choice and
it starts with the leadership. I'll wrap
up with this. AI changed what it means
to write code. It didn't change what it
means to own a codebase. The hard parts
of building software have always been
understanding the problem, making good
tradeoffs, keeping a system healthy over
time so your team can confidently build
on top of it next month and next year.
Those hard parts are still there. AI
just made them a lot easier to skip. A
function that works today, but nobody
understands tomorrow is a liability. A
code base that grows faster than your
team can reason about it is fragile. And
a dashboard that shows velocity going up
while quality is quietly going down.
That's telling your leadership a story
that isn't true. The strongest
engineering teams I see are the ones who
figured out how to use AI without giving
up the practices that make software
last. They treated generated output as a
starting point. They measure what
actually matters. They build systems
that catch quality issues early and they
hold the line on engineering standards
even when the pressure says ship faster.
The organizations that treat AI as a
shortcut are going to pay the rework tax
later. The ones that treat it as a tool
inside a strong engineering culture are
going to build software that holds up.
That's the choice sitting in front of
every team in this room. Make it on
purpose. And if you walk away with just
one thing today, make it this. Go back
to your team next week and look at your
rework rate. Look at how much time your
best people are spending on cleanup
versus new work. Have that hard
conversation because the first step to
fixing quality debt is seeing it. And
once you see it, you can't unsee it.
Thank you.
